title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series: Ep10 - Flux GGUF and Custom Nodes
description:
Welcome to the 10th episode of our ComfyUI tutorial series!

In this episode, we'll cover how to manage custom nodes in ComfyUI -adding, removing, and installing any missing ones. We'll also explore Flux GGUF, showing you how to install and use it within ComfyUI.

We’ll start by reviewing some recent updates, including fixes and new features, and then move on to managing custom nodes. You’ll learn how to install missing nodes, find alternatives when nodes cause errors, and uninstall outdated ones. Additionally, I’ll guide you through setting up Flux GGUF, including selecting the best models for your needs and optimizing performance.

We’ll also touch on practical aspects like using alternative nodes, troubleshooting common issues, and creating efficient workflows. By the end of this episode, you’ll have a deeper understanding of how to work with custom nodes and Flux GGUF, helping you make the most of ComfyUI.

If you have any questions, feel free to ask on Discord, and be sure to chec
transcript: Welcome to episode 10 of our comfy UI tutorial series.  Today, we'll talk about custom nodes,  how to add and remove them, how to install missing ones,  and some useful ones you can use.  I'll also discuss FluxGup and show you how to install  and run it on comfy UI.  Are you ready to learn?  I've opened a workflow from the last episode,  but first, let's go to the manager and check the latest news.  As you can see, they fixed some issues in the new version.  If you're on GitHub and reading discussions,  make sure you don't download files  from links containing that message.  It has fixed some memory issues in this version.  I'll go ahead and update everything,  wait for the message, and then click restart.  After the update, we'll be back to this screen.  You can see that now I have this version of the manager,  whereas before it was this version,  so if something isn't working correctly,  update your comfy UI in a day or two,  and issues might be resolved in the new version.  Let's go to the custom node manager.  You have a filter here that lets you sort the custom nodes.  If I choose install, it shows all the custom nodes  I currently have installed.  Each custom node has an ID and a title.  You can disable or uninstall a node if you want.  On the right, you can see the author, the number of stars,  and the last update.  You can sort by stars to see the more popular custom nodes  or sort by update or by ID.  In the last episode with control net,  I used an art venture node for preprocessing.  However, some users experience errors with it  and I found an alternative solution.  So this is a good opportunity to show you  how to uninstall a node.  Click on uninstall and then confirm by clicking OK.  When it's finished, you'll see a restart button.  I also want to uninstall another node  that we used in episode six  and showed an alternative for in episode seven.  So I'll uninstall the style CSV loader node as well.  By the way, if you see a warning sign,  you can click on it to view nodes that might cause conflicts.  These conflicts often arise because these nodes  have overlapping functions or names  with other nodes in your comfy UI environment.  Most of the time, you can ignore these warnings  but if you have many custom nodes, be aware of them.  You might need to disable a node  to see if that resolves issues in your workflow.  Let's restart comfy UI now that we've removed two custom nodes.  But don't worry, we'll be installing more  as the workflow becomes more complex.  So this is what it looks like when you open a workflow  and don't have some custom nodes.  In this case, I don't have the art venture custom node  because I just uninstalled it.  The node that's causing the problem  will be highlighted in red.  If you get a workflow from someone else,  it's often easy to fix by going to the manager  and clicking on install missing custom nodes.  You'll see exactly which node needs to be installed.  In this case, the art venture node.  Usually, you just click install and restart  and it should work fine.  However, in this case, I don't want the art venture node.  Instead, I'll delete it and use another node.  If I search for AIO, I can find the O pre-processor node  which is part of the node we also installed  in the last episode.  Now, I'll reconnect the image to image node  and also link it to the apply control net node.  This setup loads our image, uses the pre-processor  and then applies control net, just like I explained last time.  From the list, we can choose canny or depth,  but I'll go with canny for this case.  I'll adjust the resolution and test the workflow  by pressing Q prompt.  This is the result I get.  The advantage of this node is that it includes  many pre-processors in the list.  However, if you mostly use canny,  you can use a node that already comes with comfy UI.  I'll delete this node and show you another option for canny.  If you go to add node and the comfy UI category  under image pre-processors, you'll see we only have canny.  I wish they would add more options here,  so I wouldn't need to keep installing custom nodes.  You can select the node from this list  or double-click on the canvas, search for canny,  and select that node.  Now, I'm reconnecting image to image and testing it again.  You'll see a small red circle indicating  that the image input is missing a connection.  Let's drag a connection and test it again  as you can see it works just fine.  And you also have more settings for adjusting the canny map.  Let's load another workflow, one with styles  that we covered in episode six.  As you can see, we're getting an error  because we no longer have the node.  As I mentioned, I'm using a different node instead.  So if I go to the manager and click on install missing nodes,  you'll see the styles loader in the list.  You might install that node and still  find that the workflow doesn't work.  If that happens, click on the title of the node  to open its GitHub page.  Often, nodes require certain settings  or dependencies to function correctly,  so you need to configure them properly.  Make sure to check the node's page  when installing missing nodes.  Sometimes, a node might not appear in the missing nodes list  because the workflow creator used a custom node  that isn't available in the manager.  In that case, you'll need to find a replacement  that performs similar functions.  So I'm deleting this node and just like in episode seven,  I'll add a prompt multiple style selector, which  is part of the was node suite.  And now I can connect the positive to positive  and the negative to negative.  If I run the workflow as it is with no styles selected,  I get this image of a bunny.  Let's add acute cartoon style to see if it works correctly.  The result is this cute bunny, so it seems to work just fine.  You can find these updated workflows  in the Pixorama workflows channel in episode seven.  There, you'll find information on the nodes used  and how to configure the settings,  so the style files are recognized.  If you run into any issues, mention Pixorama on Discord.  If you have troubles with it.  If I go to the custom nodes manager and sort by installed,  you can see that I currently have five custom nodes installed,  including the manager and a few others.  However, if I go to my comfy UI folder  and then into the custom nodes folder,  you'll see all the nodes installed.  Here, I have six nodes instead of the five listed in the manager.  This is because I installed one manually  when the NF models were released.  It doesn't appear in the list.  Here, about two weeks ago, I installed that node.  But now it's marked as deprecated in favor of GUF,  which provides better results.  Since I won't be using this node anymore,  I can delete it directly from this folder  as there's no uninstall option in the manager.  I'm restarting comfy UI,  and let's go to load again to get a flux NF4 workflow  from episode eight.  Since we deleted the NF4 node, we get an error.  I'll ignore it and reconstruct this workflow for GUF instead.  Let's load an FP8 dev version workflow and use that as a base.  I'll go back to the manager  and then to the custom nodes manager, search for GUF,  and install the one by city96.  Before restarting, let's install a few more useful nodes  that I plan to use in my workflows.  Search for crystals and install that node as well  as it includes some memory monitoring tools.  Then search for RG3 and install it  as it also has some useful nodes.  Now we can restart the interface.  Let's go back to the manager and then to the custom nodes manager.  If we go to install and find the GUF node,  we just installed,  clicking on its name will open the node page.  Here you can read more about GUF and quantization  and you'll find links to the quantized models.  I'll provide links to everything you need  in the YouTube description and on Discord.  You'll see there are both dev and schnell models.  If I go to the dev models,  you'll find several versions with Q in the name.  Higher numbers generally mean higher precision,  leading to better model accuracy,  but requiring more computational resources.  You can test a few to see which offers  the best balance of speed and quality for you.  For me, the Q8 version was the best option  for both speed and quality, even though it's larger.  For schnell, which is faster but less accurate,  you might prefer the model that requires fewer steps.  For illustrations, schnell might be good,  but for realism, dev is generally better.  I'll download the Q8 version and place it  in the unit folder in Comfy UI.  Be careful to save it in the correct folder  as it shouldn't go into the checkpoints folder.  Next, we need clip models,  which help connect and understand the relationships  between text and images for better prompt understanding.  Download the Clip L model and place it in the Clip folder.  Then download the larger Clip model called T5.  You can choose from FP8 or FP16  or get a quantized version with Q in the name.  For better quality and performance,  I found that Q8 is a good option.  Download that version and place it in the Clip folder as well.  Lastly, we need a VAE as this model doesn't include VAE  and Clip.  Download the VAE model and save it in the VAE folder.  To recap, in the unit folder,  add one of the Q versions of dev or schnell.  I used the dev Q8 version.  In the Clip folder, add the Clip L model and the T5 model.  Add the VAE model to the VAE folder.  With all the models in place, let's build the workflow.  Click on Refresh to see the models  since Comfy UI was open when I downloaded them.  By the way, the Chris Tools node we installed  added some tools here to show how much memory we're using.  If you right-click on the canvas and add a node,  you can explore Comfy UI's basic and custom nodes.  This is a great way to discover what nodes are available  and experiment with them.  If you double-click on the canvas and search for a node,  you can see if it's part of Comfy UI's core installation  or from a custom node.  Nodes from custom installations will show a name on top  indicating their origin.  You can manage this by changing the badge options  in the manager.  For example, if you change the badge to none,  the title and icon will disappear,  showing that it's part of Comfy UI.  If you turn on the nickname option,  you'll see the custom node's name,  which helps keep track of which node  belongs to which custom set.  I keep this option on for clarity.  To make changes, we need to understand how it differs.  First, with the FP8 dev version, we load the checkpoint.  Then we add the text encoder with the positive prompt,  but no negative prompt since the dev version  doesn't use negatives.  Next, we use an SD-3 latent image node  and a flux guidance node, which are specific to the dev version.  For the settings on the case sampler,  start with 20 steps and always use CFG with a value of 1  to ignore the negative prompt.  After processing, the image is decoded, and you can save it.  So, to replace the load checkpoint node,  let's double-click on the canvas and search for Guff.  Since we placed our models in the UNET folder,  we'll use the UNET loader node with Guff in its name.  From the list, select your Guff model.  Since our model doesn't include the two clip models  and the VAE, we also need to load those.  Search for and add the dual-clip loader node.  I'll make sure it's the Guff version.  Here, you can select the T5 model and the Clip L model  from the list, and for type select flux.  Finally, add the load VAE node, which you've probably used  before.  This will load the flux VAE in this case.  That's the only difference.  Now, let's update the connections.  Check how the load checkpoint node was connected  and redo the connections for the three new nodes.  I'm deleting the old node, moving the new nodes up,  and connecting the model input to the model output.  Next, connect the dual-clip loader to the Clip inputs  for both positive and negative prompts.  Even though we're not using a negative prompt,  it still needs to be connected.  Connect the load VAE node to the VAE decode.  If you're using image to image,  you'll also need to connect it to the VAE encode.  That's all set.  Let's test it to see if it works.  The first generation will be slower since the model is loading,  but subsequent generations should be faster.  As you can see, it now takes only 16 seconds  to generate an image.  I'll save this workflow and others I'm working on  and add them to the Pixiroma workflows channel on Discord.  Just remember, when loading your own dev model,  make sure to select it from the list  and do the same for the Clip models.  I generated images with the full regular dev version  to compare its speed with the Q8 version.  As you can see with the Q8 model,  the first generation took 28 seconds  and subsequent generations took 16 seconds  on my RTX 4000 and 90.  In contrast, the dev model took 81 seconds  for the first generation and 20 seconds for the following ones.  In my case, Q8 was faster.  Now, I'm going to compare them, but first,  let me show you how to do that using a node from RG3,  which we installed at the beginning of this video.  We have a node called Image Compare  that I'll add to the canvas.  This node has two image inputs.  To demonstrate how it works,  I'll use two load image nodes and load two images,  Image 1 and Image 2 for just to be more clear how it works.  When you run this workflow, you'll see a tiny preview.  To make it larger, adjust the preview size.  As you move the mouse, you can compare the two images.  When the mouse is on the left, Image 1 is visible  and as you move to the right, it reveals Image 2.  The default view shows Image 1  and you can slide to the right to see Image 2.  Now, let's apply this to our workflow.  I have the dev Q8 workflow here  and I'll copy all the nodes.  Then I'll load the full dev version workflow  from episode eight and paste the Q8 nodes into it.  I'll use a fixed seed for both workflows  to compare them using the same image.  So the second workflow is Q8  and the first one is the full regular FP 16 version.  Let's add the same prompt to both workflows.  Just before the image is saved,  it's inside the VAE decode node.  We can add the image compare node.  Connect the VAE decode output to the Image 1 input  for the first workflow  and connect the second workflows via  AED decode output to the Image 2 input.  Please note, unless you have a powerful video card,  it's best not to try this at home  as loading two large models might crash comfy UI.  When I run the comparison, I get a preview  that lets me compare the two images.  This is the full version and this is the Q8 version.  Again, full version versus Q8.  As you can see, there isn't a significant difference  in quality in this case.  I haven't tested on many images,  but for me, Q8 seems good enough.  It's faster than the regular version  and also simplifies the workflow  compared to the full dev version.  You can see that the Q8 model is half the size  and the T5 clip model in the Q8 version  is also half the size.  This results in better speed  and the quality in initial tests looks similar.  So I'll go with the faster option,  but you should test it on your own computer.  For example, on an RTX 2060,  I found that the FP 8 Schnell version  was faster than the Gough one  and some of the Gough models even crashed.  It might have been a bug,  but as I mentioned, try different versions  until you find one that works well for you.  What I'm doing here is adding those art styles  to the Gough workflow.  I'll do this quickly so I can save the workflow  and add it to Discord for you.  You can watch episodes seven to see how to add the art styles  and configure the nodes so they recognize the styles.  As you can see, with the art style selected,  a simple cat can be turned into a cartoon cat  or even a ninja cat if desired.  Next, I'll rearrange the nodes to fit better on the screen  and save the workflow.  For those who prefer fewer nodes,  I'm creating a compact version.  I'm selecting all the groups right-clicking  and choosing convert group to nodes.  You'll need to experiment with this.  Just make sure you don't include all the nodes  in the group nodes as some might disconnect  during the process.  So you can always undo and try again if needed.  I usually keep the load image and save image nodes  outside the group nodes so I can resize them easily.  I hid some nodes in the group which caused disconnections  so I had to make them visible again and reconnect them.  I've done this to minimize trouble  and provide a cleaner interface,  similar to what you see in Automatic 1111 or Forge UI.  I'll add Compact to the workflow name.  Let's also test a Dev Schnelle version.  I'll download the Q8 version and place it in the models  and then the UNet folder.  Next, I'll load the Dev Q8 version workflow.  By the way, if you don't see the workflow,  it's because I saved it when it was outside  the blue rectangle showing the canvas area.  I had to move it into that area and save it again.  Back to the workflow.  Now we can switch from Dev Q8 to Schnelle Q8.  The clip models should work fine with Schnelle as well.  If you have an older video card,  try using smaller clip models until you find one  that runs smoothly.  Also, the flux guidance node doesn't work with Schnelle  so you can remove it.  I forgot to remove it during the recording  but I deleted it in the save version  for steps Schnelle only needs four  but you can set it a little higher,  like six or eight if you prefer.  When running it, the first time it took 17 seconds  to load the model and the second time,  it took under four seconds, it's quite fast.  I have the Schnelle workflow here  and have pasted the nodes from the Dev workflow  both using Q8 versions to avoid manually adding the prompt  and C to both workflows, I'll connect them.  I'll right click on the K sampler node,  choose convert widget to input  and then convert the C to input.  This will give me a C to input  where I can connect other nodes.  I'll do the same for the second workflow.  Now I'll add a primitive node.  This node adapts to whatever it's connected to  so if I connect it to the seed,  it will display the seed value.  I'll change its color to blue.  The advantage of this node is that I can connect it  to multiple workflows  so if I connect it to the second workflow,  both workflows will use the same seed  to maintain some flexibility.  I'll choose the increment option  so each time I queue a prompt,  the seed will increment by one.  If I generate something and click generate again,  the seed will be incremented by one  and if I wanna go back,  I can revert to the previous seed.  This is more useful for testing  than using random seeds.  Next, I'll do the same for the prompt.  I'll convert the text to an input in both workflows,  then add a primitive node for the prompt,  which adapts similarly.  However, since I have the comfy easy use node,  I'll just add a positive prompt  as it's already the right size and color for easy use.  I'll connect this positive prompt to both workflows  so whatever I enter here will be used  as the positive prompt in both workflows.  To compare the results,  I'll add an image compare node  and connect it to the VAE decode nodes in both workflows.  Now I can run the workflows and compare the outputs.  I'll rearrange the nodes to fit better on the screen  and add some labels for clarity.  Using the RG3 node, I'll add a label  adjusting its properties, size, and color.  I'll change the title to  Schnell Q8 for the image generated by that model  and do the same for the Dev Q8 version.  We're now ready to begin the test.  From the initial results, both workflows  produce nice images.  I think the Dev model produces more realistic results.  I use ChatGPT to generate a stable diffusion prompt  for a cute cartoon ninja in the forest.  I asked it not to include quotes  and it provided the prompt.  To make things even easier,  I requested it in a block code format  for simple copy and paste.  Once it's ready, I just click copy  and paste it into my positive prompt.  Now, the workflows are ready to run  and here are the results.  The Schnell model tends to have a few more errors  with details like hands compared to the Dev model.  By the way, on the image compare node,  if you go to the properties panel,  you have an option to choose click instead of slide.  This lets you hold the mouse button to see one version  and release it to see the other.  I prefer the slide option, so I'll switch it back.  I then ask ChatGPT for a prompt,  for a bunny wearing a samurai outfit on a hill.  I made sure that the generation was complete.  Usually, when the dot disappears, it's ready to copy.  I copied the prompt, pasted it, and ran it.  By the way, if you're wondering,  this big workflow takes 66 seconds to run.  Since I added Guff, I'm seeing this warning.  If anyone knows of a fix, please let me know.  It hasn't affected the speed, but I don't like seeing errors.  Here are the results for the bunny prompt.  Both versions look interesting.  Which version is your favorite?  Let's also try some portraits to see how both models  handle them.  I asked for glasses, curly hair, and a white blouse.  Here are the results.  Schnell usually has more vivid colors in contrast,  while dev tends to be more realistic.  I'll also request a Viking prompt,  specifying a beard and a dramatic, ready to fight look.  Here are the results.  Schnell struggles a bit with holding objects  and has a more illustrative style, while dev  is more realistic, though not perfect.  I'll make it a bit more challenging for Flux  by asking for a gameplay menu with a Pixel Roma logo on top  and three different buttons, each with its own text underneath.  As expected, Schnell is almost there,  but messed up the continue and exit buttons.  Dev did a very good job, but included an extra button.  With a few more generations, I'm confident you can get  perfectly out with all the buttons just right.  It's impressive that this can be achieved with these models.  Let's try a dessert prompt, a chocolate cake  with a strawberry on top, and add Pixel Roma dessert  on a napkin.  Both models managed it, but Dev did a beautiful job,  getting all the text on the napkin right,  and the strawberry looks great.  The result is just delicious.  Let's try a vector style design this time,  a gnome silhouette on a white background.  I keep having issues with Dev where I get blurred images.  It seems to happen with certain seeds,  especially when I use prompts for vector styles  or specify a white background.  I've tried different samplers and schedulers,  but no matter what I try, the images are still blurry  with some seeds.  If anyone finds a solution, please let me know.  If we go to the manager and then to the custom nodes manager,  you can search for specific nodes,  but the search functionality is somewhat limited.  It might help you find nodes if their description  includes the keywords you're searching for.  However, there's a website that provides  a better search experience for nodes.  On the comfy.icu website, under the resources section,  you'll find a page for custom nodes.  Here, you can search for nodes more effectively.  For example, if you search for compare,  you'll find the RG3 node I use for image comparison.  By clicking on it, you can access more information  and even visit the nodes, GitHub page  for detailed usage instructions.  This tool can be quite useful for finding  and learning about different custom nodes. With the creator account, I get approximately two hours  of voice credits per month, but that's often not enough  since sometimes you need to generate or regenerate content  for the extra credits beyond the monthly limit.  It's costing me extra.  As you can see, I've used a total amount for the month  and for any additional credits I have to pay extra,  it costs $0.30 for 1,000 credits.  For today's extra credits, I had to spend $24.  On top of the $22 I've already paid  to help cover these costs, I created a membership program. There are four different membership levels,  starting at just $2, $3, and you'll get access  to the badges, stickers, and members only videos.  The legend level also includes access  to a special legend-only Discord channel.  If you connect your Discord to YouTube,  you'll automatically get a role on my Discord server  with specific access and priority replies.  To connect your Discord account,  go to your Discord profile settings,  click on connections, add YouTube, and follow the steps.  As you can see, mine is already connected.  Don't forget to check out the PixelRoma workflows  and other useful channels.  If you encounter any issues,  you can mention PixelRoma on any public channel  and I'll help if I can.  And yes, I just hit the 10,000 subscribers milestone.  Thank you all for your support.  Don't forget to subscribe to my other channel,  AI2Play as well.  That's all for today.  If you enjoyed this, please leave a like,  or a comment, and I'll see you in the next episode.  Have a great day.
